data.title: Why Wall Street Is Turning More Bullish On Nvidia Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is heading into its next earnings report with expectations already running high, and Wall Street appears increasingly comfortable with that risk. Several analysts, led by Citigroup, have reiterated bullish views on the stock, arguing that demand for advanced AI hardware remains strong enough to support another leg of growth.

For investors, the key question is no longer whether Nvidia will beat earnings estimates. It is whether guidance can justify a valuation built on the assumption that AI spending remains durable through 2026 and beyond.

Why Analysts Are Staying Positive

Citi's recent commentary reflects a broader pattern across sell side research. Analysts continue to point to sustained orders for Nvidia's newest data center platforms, including systems built around its latest GPU architecture and high speed networking products.

Unlike earlier in the AI cycle, when demand was concentrated among a few U.S. hyperscalers, the customer base now appears wider. Enterprise adoption, government backed projects, and international infrastructure builds are increasingly part of the revenue mix. That diversification helps support forecasts that data center revenue can remain elevated even if cloud spending growth slows in the United States.

Citi has also emphasized that Nvidia's product roadmap provides visibility that many competitors lack. New chip platforms and system level upgrades give customers reasons to refresh hardware rather than simply sweat existing assets.

From an earnings perspective, that matters because it keeps average selling prices high and supports gross margins.

Guidance Matters More Than The Quarter

At this stage of Nvidia's rally, investors are less focused on what happened last quarter and more focused on what management says about the next several.

The stock's valuation assumes that AI infrastructure spending is not peaking but shifting into a longer buildout phase. That puts pressure on Nvidia's guidance to show that order pipelines remain strong and that customers are planning capacity expansions rather than pauses.

If management signals that backlog remains healthy and that next generation systems are ramping as expected, analysts are likely to maintain or raise long term revenue estimates. That scenario supports the bullish case that Nvidia's growth story extends well into 2026.

However, any hint that customers are delaying purchases or that supply chain constraints are limiting shipments could trigger a sharper market reaction than a simple earnings miss would.

In this environment, tone and forward commentary may move the stock more than headline numbers.

How This Fits Into The AI Spending Cycle

Nvidia's earnings have become a proxy for the broader AI investment cycle. When it beats expectations, markets tend to read that as confirmation that capital spending on data centers and AI systems remains strong.

Citi's optimism is rooted in the idea that AI workloads are still in an early stage of enterprise adoption. Training large models is no longer the only driver. Inference, automation, and industry specific AI applications are creating ongoing demand for compute power.

That narrative suggests that spending is evolving rather than fading. Instead of one time bursts tied to model launches, Nvidia could benefit from recurring upgrades as companies scale AI into everyday operations.

For investors, this distinction is important. A one off cycle would justify lower multiples. A multi year infrastructure build supports premium valuations.

Valuation Risk And Market Sensitivity

Even with positive analyst sentiment, Nvidia's stock remains sensitive to any sign of deceleration. The higher a stock climbs, the less room there is for disappointment.

Citi's bullish stance does not eliminate the risk that expectations have become too optimistic. Revenue growth has been extraordinary, which raises the bar for future comparisons. Any slowdown, even if growth remains strong in absolute terms, could pressure the share price.

There is also the question of competition. While Nvidia dominates high end AI chips today, rivals are investing heavily in alternative accelerators and custom silicon. Over time, that could compress margins or limit pricing power.

For now, analysts appear comfortable that Nvidia's software ecosystem and integration advantages give it a defensible lead. But earnings calls will increasingly be judged on how well management explains its competitive moat.

What Traders And Long Term Investors Should Watch

In the short term, traders will focus on guidance and management's commentary on customer demand. Signs that large buyers are still placing multi quarter orders would likely be taken as bullish.

Long term investors may pay closer attention to capital allocation and product roadmaps. Continued investment in next generation platforms signals confidence in future demand. Any shift toward caution could change the narrative.

Another factor to watch is geographic expansion. If management highlights growth outside the United States, that supports the idea that AI infrastructure spending is becoming global rather than concentrated in a few tech hubs.

That would reinforce the argument that Nvidia's growth runway is longer than many skeptics assume.

Why It Matters Now

Citi's bullish view ahead of earnings reflects a broader belief on Wall Street that Nvidia is still in the middle of its AI expansion phase, not at the end of it.

The upcoming earnings report will test that belief. If guidance confirms that demand for AI systems remains strong and diversified, Nvidia's valuation may continue to look justified to investors focused on long term growth.

If guidance falls short of that narrative, the stock could face a period of consolidation as expectations reset.

Either way, this earnings report is less about one quarter of results and more about whether Nvidia can keep convincing the market that AI spending is structural, global, and durable rather than temporary and concentrated.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Opendoor Stock In Focus Ahead Of Q4 Earnings: What To Know
data.description:
Opendoor Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:OPEN) stock is in the spotlight Thursday ahead of the company’s fourth-quarter earnings report after the market closes.

  • Opendoor shares are under pressure. Why are OPEN shares declining?

Earnings Expectations, Recent Results And What To Watch

Opendoor is expected to report a loss of 10 cents per share. The company has beaten revenue estimates in two of the last four quarters and topped earnings per share estimates in three of the last four quarters. In its most recent quarter, Opendoor posted a loss of 8 cents per share, missing the consensus estimate for a 7-cent loss. Revenue came in at $915.0 million, ahead of the $849.59 million estimate.

“Our path to profitability is clear: transact with more sellers, strengthen our unit economics through better pricing and resale speed, and drive operational efficiency by being ruthless on expenses. By the end of next year, we will drive Opendoor to breakeven Adjusted Net Income on a 12-month go-forward basis,” said Kaz Nejatian, CEO of Opendoor, in the company’s third-quarter earnings release.

Investors should keep an eye on Opendoor’s transaction volume, which is a key indicator of market demand and operational efficiency. Additionally, monitoring the average home sale price could provide insights into the company’s pricing strategy and market positioning. Lastly, watch for any changes in customer acquisition costs, as these metrics will be critical in determining the company’s ability to scale effectively in a competitive environment.

Bearish Momentum Persists Despite Long-Term Gains

Opendoor is currently trading at $4.50, which is 11.9% below its 20-day SMA and significantly below its longer-term 50-day and 100-day SMAs by 23.7% and 32.2%, respectively. This positioning indicates a bearish trend over both short and medium terms. Over the past 12 months, the stock has seen a substantial increase of 206.04%.

The RSI stands at 38.08, suggesting that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold, but leans towards a bearish momentum. The MACD further supports this bearish outlook, indicating downward momentum.

Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for Opendoor Technologies, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Momentum: Bullish (Score: 96.54) — The stock is outperforming the broader market significantly in terms of momentum.

The Verdict: Opendoor’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a strong momentum trend. This suggests that despite the current price challenges, there is underlying strength in market activity that could bode well for future performance.

Opendoor Shares Edge Lower

OPEN Price Action: At the time of writing, Opendoor stock is trading 0.43% lower at $4.60, according to data from Benzinga Pro.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Broadstone Net Lease Posts Q4 Beat, But Leverage Expansion Deserves A Closer Look
data.description:
Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (NYSE:BNL) reported fourth-quarter 2025 results on February 18 that exceeded Street estimates on both revenue and funds from operations, while reaffirming its 2026 outlook. The quarter concluded a year in which the industrial-focused net lease REIT returned to growth — yet the balance sheet presents a more layered picture.

What Happened

BNL posted Q4 revenue of $118.3 million, above the $116.4 million consensus. Core FFO reached $0.39 per diluted share versus the $0.37 Street estimate. Adjusted funds from operations came in at $0.38 per share, in line with the FactSet projection.

For the full year, AFFO totaled $1.49 per diluted share, a 4.2% increase from $1.43 in 2024. The company reaffirmed 2026 AFFO guidance of $1.53 to $1.57 per share — unchanged from the range issued in December — while narrowing its core general and administrative expense target to $30–$31 million from the prior $30.5–$31.5 million.

The Structural Picture

Operational metrics remained stable, but the leverage trajectory can warrant closer examination.

BNL ended 2025 with Net Debt to Annualized Adjusted EBITDAre of 6.0x — up from 5.0x at the end of 2024. Total outstanding debt rose to $2.5 billion from $1.9 billion a year earlier, a roughly 31% increase. The expansion was facilitated by $748.4 million in total investments during 2025, funded in part through a new $500 million unsecured term loan and a $350 million senior unsecured notes offering at a 5.00% coupon due 2032.

Two structural offsets merit consideration. First, the company has no material debt maturities until 2027, which removes near-term refinancing pressure. Second, the revolver retained $723.5 million of available capacity at year-end, providing a liquidity layer against further capital deployment.

The build-to-suit pipeline can add another dimension. BNL has $174.8 million in remaining estimated investments across nine in-process developments, all targeted for completion by late 2026. These projects carry a weighted average estimated cash cap rate of 7.4% and a straight-line yield of 8.7% — economics that could contribute to EBITDA growth, which may influence leverage metrics over time as projects reach stabilization.

What Else Stands Out

BNL raised its quarterly dividend by 0.9% to $0.2925 per share. The increase is consistent with the AFFO growth trajectory reported for the year.

Capital recycling continued at a measured pace: $96.1 million in gross disposition proceeds from 28 properties during 2025 at a weighted average cap rate of 7.3%. The 2026 guidance assumes $75–$100 million in dispositions and $500–$625 million in new investments, indicating a continuation of the net-investment-positive posture.

On the tenant credit front, BNL’s largest tenant accounts for 3.9% of annualized base rent, and the portfolio’s ABR-weighted average lease term stands at 9.6 years with 2.1% average annual escalations — both characteristics that can contribute to rating stability for BBB- issuers.

Why It Matters

BNL’s Q4 results indicate that the operational framework is executing as projected: rents are expanding, occupancy remains near-full, and the development pipeline is producing yields of 8.7%. The structural question is whether the pace of capital deployment — and the leverage it has required — remains within the tolerances of a BBB- balance sheet.

With no material maturities until 2027 and embedded growth from the build-to-suit portfolio, BNL appears to have runway. Whether that runway translates into improved leverage metrics over time could shape the credit profile ahead of the next rating review cycle.

Sources: BNL Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2025 Earnings Release (February 18, 2026); BNL Business Update and 2026 AFFO Guidance (December 1, 2025)

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: $100 Invested In State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF 15 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:
State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (NYSE:SPYG) has outperformed the market over the past 15 years by 2.27% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 13.85%. Currently, State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF has a market capitalization of $43.88 billion.

Buying $100 In SPYG: If an investor had bought $100 of SPYG stock 15 years ago, it would be worth $708.45 today based on a price of $103.75 for SPYG at the time of writing.

State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF's Performance Over Last 15 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

data.title: Sam Bankman-Fried Alleges Judge Uses 'Trump Playbook' On Him To Block Evidence
data.description:
Sam Bankman-Fried said an appeals court just overturned a conviction because Judge Lewis Kaplan wrongfully blocked evidence—the same judge who sentenced SBF to 25 years and presided over Trump’s E. Jean Carroll case.

The Appeals Court Precedent

The Second Circuit threw out a conviction in a separate case, finding that Judge Kaplan wrongfully excluded evidence of intent. 

The appeals court vacated several counts and remanded for further proceedings—a significant rebuke of Kaplan’s evidentiary decisions.

Bankman-Fried immediately connected the dots. “Kaplan repeatedly cut off my testimony and excluded every kind of evidence the government asked him to,” he posted on X. 

His pending Second Circuit appeal argues the same exclusion of intent evidence violated his right to a fair trial.

The excluded evidence falls into several categories: lawyers approved the entities and bank accounts FTX used, lawyers drafted the Payment Agent Agreement between FTX and Alameda Research, lawyers approved loans to executives, and lawyers drafted FTX’s terms of service governing customer funds.

The Intent Argument

SBF’s defense is straightforward: if attorneys reviewed, drafted, and blessed these arrangements, he couldn’t have believed he was acting illegally. 

In criminal fraud cases, the government must prove willful intent. Without showing legal sign-off, the jury lacked context to evaluate that element.

Judge Kaplan sentenced SBF to 25 years in federal prison in March 2024, calling it one of the most significant financial fraud cases in U.S. history. 

However, the defense argues the jury only heard “one side of the story” because Kaplan excluded evidence showing lawyers approved key business practices.

During November 2025 oral arguments, Second Circuit Judge Barrington Parker appeared skeptical, asking whether attorney testimony would have genuinely changed the verdict given overwhelming evidence presented at trial. 

However, the new ruling in a separate Kaplan case gives SBF’s team fresh ammunition.

The Trump Connection

Bankman-Fried escalated his attack by linking Kaplan’s conduct to the judge’s handling of cases involving Trump—specifically the E. Jean Carroll civil litigation where Kaplan presided.

“He used the same playbook on @realDonaldTrump,” SBF posted.

The political timing is notable. Trump recently stated he has no intention of granting Bankman-Fried clemency. 

By aligning himself rhetorically with Trump as a victim of the same judge, SBF appears to play to a broader audience while his legal team pursues the narrower appellate argument.

Image: Shutterstock
